text: Today we're going to discuss a case of a robotic repair of an incarcerated right-sided diaphragmatic hernia after liver resection.
There are no conflicts of interest and we have nothing to disclose.
Right-sided diaphragmatic hernias are most often due to blunt or penetrating trauma.
However, they are a complication of liver resection and liver transplant in approximately two to three percent of patients.
They have a high risk of incarceration and strangulation, so all right-sided diaphragmatic hernias should be repaired regardless of symptoms.
Our case was a forty-three-year-old female who was three years status post large hepatic adenoma resection.
She presented to the emergency room with several months of abdominal discomfort and diarrhea, with acutely worsening pain over the last several days.
She now had eight out of ten constant diffuse abdominal pain and nausea.
As mentioned, past medical history seems for hepatic adenoma resection
hypothyroidism, anxiety, and depression.
She's otherwise a non-smoker and a social alcohol user.
On physical exam, her vitals were stable in the emergency department.
On abdominal exam, she was soft, non-distended, tender to palpation in the right upper quadrant, and she had her prior chevron incision scar.
Her labs were otherwise unremarkable.
A CAT scan of the abdomen and pelvis was obtained with IV contrasts.
which clearly showed a right-sided diaphragmatic hernia near the old hepatic adenoma resection site.
The hernia sac is visualized containing the transverse colon, which appears distended proximally and decompressed distally, suggesting an obstruction within the colon.
The video displays a coronal view of the incarcerated hernia, showing the anatomical structures involved and their spatial orientation.
The sagittal view reveals the incarcerated hernia, showing its spatial orientation and relationship to surrounding anatomical structures.
The patient was admitted to the hospital.
with plans to proceed with a robotic repair of the hernia.

The surgical team places a twelve millimeter Hassan port in the right upper quadrant, along with three eight millimeter robotic ports: two on the left side along the old chevron incision and one in the epigastric area.
The surgeon lyses adhesions from the patient's prior surgery to gain exposure to the hernia site, using robotic instruments visible through the ports placed earlier.
However, this was left out in the interest of time.
The surgical team visualizes the hernia through the robotic-assisted laparoscopic setup, with ports placed in the right upper quadrant, left side, and epigastric area.
The diaphragm is visible with the transverse colon and omentum protruding through the diaphragm defect.
The surgeon uses scissors attached to a monopolar electrocautery to dissect the anterior portion of the hernia sac, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The transverse colon and omentum are visibly protruding through the diaphragm defect and cannot be reduced back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon opens the diaphragm to facilitate the reduction of the hernia, revealing the hernia contents protruding through the diaphragm defect.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make an incision in the diaphragm, facilitating the reduction of herniated structures.
The surgeon continues to open the diaphragm, resulting in the loss of pneumoperitoneum as air visibly escapes into the pleural space.
A diamond flex liver retractor is placed through the assistant port to elevate the diaphragm, enhancing visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon visualizes the hernia defect while continuing to open the diaphragm using a harmonic scalpel, revealing the hernia contents including the transverse colon and omentum.
The surgeon reduces the hernia contents back into the abdominal cavity after enlarging the defect in the diaphragm, with visible movement of the hernia contents as they are repositioned.
The surgeon reduces the transverse colon and omentum back into the abdominal cavity, following the enlargement of the hernia defect.
The surgeon has successfully reduced all abdominal contents back into the abdominal cavity, with the hernia defect visible and the diaphragm opened.
The surgeon visually assesses the hernia defect, which measures approximately five centimeters in length.
The surgeon exposes the lung through the diaphragmatic defect, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a secondary defect lateral to the primary diaphragmatic defect, visibly containing incarcerated omentum.
The surgeon completes the reduction of the transverse colon and omentum from the hernia defect, ensuring all abdominal contents are returned to their proper position.
The surgeon uses a PEDS V-lock suture to perform a primary repair of the visible diaphragmatic defect.
The surgeon begins anteriorly to elevate the diaphragmatic defect upwards, improving visualization of the surgical area.
The surgeon takes imbricated bites with a suture to reinforce the closure of the diaphragmatic defect, ensuring a strong repair.
The surgeon moves the liver tract inferiorly to visualize the inferior aspect of the diaphragmatic defect, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hernia repair.
The surgeon reinforces the diaphragmatic hernia repair by placing an 8 cm by 5 cm Allomax mesh over the repaired area, addressing multiple diaphragmatic defects in the young patient.
The surgeon selects a biological mesh for reinforcement due to the presence of the incarcerated transverse colon, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to anchor the Allomax mesh in a circumferential manner around the diaphragmatic defect, ensuring secure placement of the mesh.
The hernia defect is visibly closed with the mesh securely in place, demonstrating the completion of the repair.
Postoperatively, the diet was advanced on postoperative day one and patient was discharged home on postoperative day two.
In conclusion, right side diaphragmatic hernia is commonly seen after trauma, liver resection, and liver transplants.
These hernias should be repaired and surgically fit patients due to high risk of incarceration and strangulation.
Peer repair can be approached robotically through the abdomen, with or without mesh, depending on the clinical situation, such as hernia size, multiplicity of defects, and age of patient.
